caveats: a: OEWS provides occupation shares rather than task shares and excludes self-employed workers.
a: Exposure differs between standardized volume production, custom fine jewelry, components, and silverware.
a: Creative assistance does not imply that marketable design judgment is fully substitutable.
rho: BLS identifies technology use and projected productivity, not AI implementation rates.
rho: Anthropic usage is vendor-specific and not a representative manufacturing sample.
rho: Implementation will vary with digital asset quality and brand or product complexity.
e: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed eligibility audit.
e: NAICS boundaries distinguish manufacturing from retail and wholesale, but business databases may not.
e: Precious-metal and stone inventory accounting can distort normalized EBITDA and apparent scale.
s5: The Census proxy is all-industry, response-based, and has data year 2018.
s5: Owner age does not establish sale intent or business transferability.
s5: Founder-linked aesthetics or customer relationships can cause retirement to produce closure rather than sale.
q: No source directly measures retention of AI-created gross benefit.
q: Material value can dominate price and obscure retained labor savings.
q: Pricing power differs sharply between branded design, custom work, private label, components, and silverware.
d5: PCE includes watches and imports and is not a domestic production series.
d5: Chain-dollar movements may not fully isolate shifts between natural and laboratory-grown stones or product quality.
d5: The category is sensitive to recession, wealth, commodity prices, and fashion.
o: No direct source measures the future share requiring a US LMM manufacturer.
o: Imports may replace the lens operator even when consumer demand remains intact.
o: Operator need varies between unique fine jewelry and standardized components or silverware.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Manufacturers sell finished collections, custom pieces, components, findings, stones, and silverware through wholesale accounts, brands, retailers, and sometimes direct channels. Revenue is unit and order based, with repeat seasonal collections and replenishment rather than contractual subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure jewelry retailers, wholesalers without manufacturing, repair-only shops, plating-only businesses, synthetic-stone manufacturers, captive workshops, offshore-only producers without a US operating manufacturer, shells, inactive entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying jewelry, precious-metal personal goods, silverware, lapidary work, unassembled jewelry parts, findings, and related finished products to external wholesale, retail, brand, or direct customers.

a.bridge: BLS directly measures the NAICS 339910 occupation mix but not task-level AI exposure. Production occupations are 58.81% of employment, including 29.61% jewelers and precious-stone workers and 7.29% inspectors; office and administrative support is 14.16%, sales is 8.11%, and design is 2.53%. I assign greater exposure to design ideation and iteration, costing, product descriptions, customer service, order processing, inventory planning, marketing, and administration, with lower exposure for casting, stone setting, polishing, inspection, and handling. Wage weighting raises exposed management and commercial work, while the occupation titles may hide digitally assisted tasks inside production; this makes direction of error mixed.
caveats: OEWS provides occupation shares rather than task shares and excludes self-employed workers.
Exposure differs between standardized volume production, custom fine jewelry, components, and silverware.
Creative assistance does not imply that marketable design judgment is fully substitutable.
a.change_evidence: Obtain establishment-level payroll by occupation and conduct time-and-task studies across design, merchandising, sales, order management, purchasing, production planning, bench work, gemology, and inspection at fifteen manufacturers segmented by automation level.
a.rationale: AI can generate and refine concepts, prepare variants and product content, assist costing and sourcing, forecast replenishment, answer routine customer questions, and automate administrative workflows. Manual dexterity, material judgment, gem verification, casting, soldering, setting, polishing, repair, and final inspection remain central.

d5.bridge: BEA's real personal consumption expenditures cover US jewelry and watches purchased from all domestic and foreign suppliers, not constant-quality demand for the current US manufacturing basket. Real spending was $93.152 billion in 2025, below $94.007 billion in 2021 and $95.152 billion in 2022 but above 2019. I interpret this as a mature, cyclical category with roughly flat central demand, then adjust the downside for imports and the upside for customization and direct channels. Consumer PCE overstates domestic-manufacturer demand when import share rises and understates B2B components or nonconsumer silverware.
caveats: PCE includes watches and imports and is not a domestic production series.
Chain-dollar movements may not fully isolate shifts between natural and laboratory-grown stones or product quality.
The category is sensitive to recession, wealth, commodity prices, and fashion.
d5.change_evidence: Combine BEA PCE with Census manufacturing shipments, USITC import volumes, Jewelers Board of Trade sales, wedding and luxury-channel data, and manufacturer orders by product; deflate metal, stone, and quality mix separately.
d5.rationale: Jewelry demand is supported by gifting, weddings, self-purchase, personalization, and replacement, but it is discretionary and exposed to real-income cycles, fashion changes, high precious-material prices, resale, and imports. Recent real spending supports stability rather than a strong structural-growth assumption.

caveats: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed eligibility audit.
NAICS boundaries distinguish manufacturing from retail and wholesale, but business databases may not.
Precious-metal and stone inventory accounting can distort normalized EBITDA and apparent scale.
e.change_evidence: Audit all 149 candidates using Census classifications, D&B/Hoovers, Jewelers Board of Trade, state registrations, facility evidence, websites, import records, ownership, customer channels, and five-year normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The frozen count already targets the LMM band, and genuine manufacturers typically sell repeatedly to external wholesale, retail, brand, or direct customers. Eligibility falls for vertically integrated captive workshops, entities that are actually retailers or wholesalers, repair and plating businesses, offshore brokers, inactive firms, artisan practices without transferable systems, and companies whose profitability is outside the band after normalizing precious-material price swings.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 339910 ($1,339,174,782) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 339910 ($8,791,426,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $441,802 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 339910); class <5: 1,245 firms, avg revenue/firm $338,481 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $66,410 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 295 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,684,783 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $330,554 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 141 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,809,255 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $747,376 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 149 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,310,577 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,611,535 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $84,364,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,552,282 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $319,505,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,686,995 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 149

caveats: No direct source measures the future share requiring a US LMM manufacturer.
Imports may replace the lens operator even when consumer demand remains intact.
Operator need varies between unique fine jewelry and standardized components or silverware.
o.change_evidence: Measure domestic versus imported unit volumes, retailer private-label sourcing, direct CAD-to-contract-manufacturer flows, in-house additive manufacturing, resale substitution, and lost-bid reasons using USITC, customs, retailer, and manufacturer data.
o.rationale: Physical jewelry, components, stones, and silverware still require accountable fabrication, material handling, finishing, inspection, and truthful product representation. Software can automate design and commerce but cannot deliver the finished physical object. Domestic operators can nevertheless lose quantity to imports, retailer-controlled production, consumer or retailer additive manufacturing, resale, and product substitution.

caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-created gross benefit.
Material value can dominate price and obscure retained labor savings.
Pricing power differs sharply between branded design, custom work, private label, components, and silverware.
q.change_evidence: Review five years of SKU- and customer-level prices, metal and stone pass-through clauses, wholesale contracts, discounts, returns, scrap, labor standards, and margins at twenty manufacturers; interview retail and brand procurement teams.
q.rationale: Branded, differentiated, custom, and rapid-turn products can retain design, planning, and overhead savings through product pricing. Commodity components and private-label production face retailer bargaining, imports, transparent metal and stone costs, and rapid diffusion of CAD and generative tools. FTC truthfulness and disclosure obligations also limit unattended content automation and create costly downside from errors.

rho.bridge: Anthropic measures automation patterns and context constraints across enterprise API users, not jewelry manufacturers, while BLS documents existing CAD/CAM, 3D printing, lasers, and robotic polishing in jewelry work. I translate the existing digital-design and additive-manufacturing substrate into implementation for exposed design, content, planning, and administrative tasks, then reduce for fragmented product data, tacit aesthetic judgment, valuable-material controls, provenance, disclosure, and physical quality checks. API adopters are more digitally mature than typical LMM manufacturers, biasing the generic automation rate upward.
caveats: BLS identifies technology use and projected productivity, not AI implementation rates.
Anthropic usage is vendor-specific and not a representative manufacturing sample.
Implementation will vary with digital asset quality and brand or product complexity.
rho.change_evidence: Measure live AI and CAD workflow adoption, design-cycle time, approval rates, forecast accuracy, content error rates, scrap, rework, and labor hours across fifteen LMM manufacturers and their ERP, PIM, ecommerce, and CAD systems.
rho.rationale: Digital catalogs, CAD files, product images, bills of material, and order histories support practical AI deployment. Human approval remains necessary for brand coherence, wearability, manufacturability, gem identity, metal content, provenance, and high-value material release.

s5.bridge: Census measures age among 4.1 million responding owners of US employer businesses in 2018, not qualifying control transfers among eligible jewelry manufacturers. I use the 51% age-55-or-older share as succession pressure and discount it for five-year timing, family or employee succession, brand or designer dependence, continued ownership, closures, and strategic transactions outside the lens. Craft manufacturing may have older proprietors than the employer-business average, biasing the proxy downward, while owner age alone substantially overstates completed external sales.
caveats: The Census proxy is all-industry, response-based, and has data year 2018.
Owner age does not establish sale intent or business transferability.
Founder-linked aesthetics or customer relationships can cause retirement to produce closure rather than sale.
s5.change_evidence: Match verified candidates to ten years of PitchBook, GF Data, Capital IQ, Axial, Jewelers Board of Trade, and state control records, then survey owners on age, family succession, sale intent, brand ownership, and expected timing.
s5.rationale: Long-tenured owner-designers and family businesses create plausible succession supply, but transferability depends on brand rights, design archives, customer accounts, sourcing relationships, inventory controls, and a team that can operate without the founder.

principal_driver: Existing CAD, additive manufacturing, and rich product data create practical AI leverage across design-to-order workflows.
principal_weakness: Mature discretionary demand and import-heavy price competition make durable retention of widely available efficiency gains uncertain.

sources.supports: Defines the primary activity as jewelry and silverware manufacturing, including lapidary work and unassembled jewelry parts and stock products, while distinguishing retail, wholesale, repair, plating, and synthetic-stone activities.
sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form MC-33991

sources.supports: Exact-industry occupation mix: production 58.81%, jewelers and precious-stone workers 29.61%, inspectors 7.29%, office and administrative support 14.16%, sales 8.11%, and designers 2.22% of employment.
sources.title: Jewelry and Silverware Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: Describes manual and digital duties, including CAD/CAM, 3D printing, lasers, setting and polishing; projects employment down 5% from 2024 to 2034 as automation raises productivity and imports reduce domestic demand.
sources.title: Jewelers and Precious Stone and Metal Workers

sources.supports: BEA annual real PCE via FRED shows $93.152 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2025, compared with $94.007 billion in 2021, $95.152 billion in 2022, and $70.904 billion in 2019.
sources.title: Real Personal Consumption Expenditures: Durable Goods: Jewelry and Watches

sources.supports: Census 2019 Annual Business Survey graphic for data year 2018 shows 51% of responding employer-business owners age 55 or older, 43% age 35 to 54, and 6% age 34 or younger.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: Reports 77% automation patterns in first-party API business usage and identifies organized contextual information and data modernization as constraints on sophisticated enterprise deployment.
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Uneven geographic and enterprise AI adoption

sources.supports: States marketers must truthfully represent jewelry type, grade, quality, metallic content, size, weight, treatment, origin, value, production, manufacture, and related characteristics, supporting human review and disclosure constraints.
sources.title: Jewelry Guides

title: Jewelry and Silverware Manufacturing
